hero_eyebrow: AI, explained like a normal person would
hero_sub_html: No fanfare. No India launch event. Just the first frontier model that doesn't lose the plot when you switch languages mid-sentence. Skim it in 5, or go deep in 12 — your call.

tldr.label: Big one
tldr.body: Claude Opus 4.8 quietly clears a reasoning benchmark by 60% — and finally handles Hinglish without dropping context.

tldr.label: Build Notes
tldr.body: Why your fine-tuned model gets worse after every retraining — and the cheaper fix nobody runs.

tldr.label: Jobs
tldr.body: A real number on AI-eval roles in India, plus the senior-IC question doing the rounds this month.

tldr.label: Reality
tldr.body: What an English-trained frontier model still drops the moment you write the way India actually talks.

one_thing.head: Claude 4.8 is the first frontier model that doesn't break on Hinglish
one_thing.lede_html: Everyone clipped the headline number: Claude Opus 4.8 jumped 60% on a new reasoning benchmark. Big, real, worth a tweet. The quieter result showed up by Wednesday — Indian devs running it on the same Hinglish prompts that broke GPT-4o and Gemini Pro found that 4.8 held the thread. Code-switching mid-sentence, English noun + Hindi verb, mixed Devanagari and roman script — the kinds of prompts your engineers actually write to it. It's not Indic-specialised; it just stopped failing in the way every other frontier model fails. Quiet, huge, and almost nobody covered it.

skip_list.title: Skip List
skip_list.body: Skip this week: hot takes calling 4.8 "a new era". Why: it's a real upgrade, not a phase shift — treat it like a tool, not a moment.

rotation_note.aside: Everyone gets a turn — no lens is filler.

lenses.label: Builder
lenses.caption: You ship the thing
lenses.body_html: If your product takes Indian-user input, run your top 20 production prompts through 4.8 this weekend. The wins will be where you've been hacking — translation pre-passes, language detection, transliteration. Most of that scaffolding is now net cost, not net help.
lenses.action: → Do: A/B 4.8 vs your current model on 50 real Hinglish queries. Cut the pre-translation step where 4.8 wins on its own.

lenses.label: Product / Biz
lenses.caption: You decide the roadmap
lenses.body_html: The Indic-language gap was a strategic lever for some Indian products — "we work for the next-billion users, they don't". 4.8 narrows that gap, doesn't close it. Update your competitive frame: Indic-fluency is now a tier, not a moat. The defensible play moves one layer up — proprietary Indian data, distribution, trust.
lenses.action: → This quarter: rewrite your one-pager. Replace "Indic-language advantage" with the specific data, partner, or workflow you own that 4.8 can't touch.

lenses.label: Secure Pro
lenses.caption: Job's safe, day's changing
lenses.body_html: Your team will read the 4.8 numbers and want to rip up the roadmap. Don't — not yet. If you're mid-career, the habit worth starting is a "tried, kept, deferred" note per quarter — one line for what 4.8 lets you ship now, one for what stays as-is, one for what you'll reassess next quarter. If you're senior, the harder move is the "not this quarter" list — three things 4.8 makes tempting but not yet wise. Mine for your team: (1) Ripping out the translation/transliteration layer — keep it for one more quarter; 4.8 still degrades on long mixed-script prompts. (2) Re-running the Indic-language eval suite from scratch — your existing one is fine, add 4.8 as a column. (3) Switching default model for production traffic — pilot on internal tools first, watch latency and cost per call before you flip customers. That one-pager is your contribution this week. It's worth more than any prototype.
lenses.action: → Mid-career: write the "tried, kept, deferred" one-pager — one line each — by Friday. Senior: write the 3-item "not this quarter" list and the one sentence you'd defend it with in the CTO review. Send by Monday.

lenses.label: Switcher
lenses.caption: Want into AI, minus the fear
lenses.body_html: If you speak any Indian language, you have a real, undervalued skill right now: spotting where frontier models still break for Indian users. You don't need to code to do this — you need to test.
lenses.action: → First rung: run 10 Hinglish prompts (the way you'd actually type to a friend) through Claude 4.8 and one other model. Note where each breaks. That's a portfolio artifact — "India-eval auditor" — built in 45 min.

build_notes.title: Your fine-tuned model gets worse after every retraining. Here's why — and the cheaper fix.
build_notes.paper_ref: ▸ unpacked from: Kotha et al., "Understanding Catastrophic Forgetting in Sequential Fine-Tuning" (NeurIPS 2025) + the LoRA-merging follow-ups
build_notes.skim_html: The 20-second version: every time you fine-tune on this month's data, the model quietly forgets last month's. It's called catastrophic forgetting — and full fine-tuning makes it worse than LoRA-style adapters. Fix: stop full-retraining monthly. Run a small LoRA per data slice and merge at inference. Ship metric: evaluate every new fine-tune on the oldest eval set you have, not just the freshest.
build_notes.struggle_html: You ship a fine-tune. It nails this month's queries. Next quarter you fine-tune again on new data — the new stuff works, but a chunk of last quarter's wins quietly stop working. Users notice. You don't, until they complain.
build_notes.finding_html: Full fine-tuning rewrites the same weights every cycle, so each pass overwrites the previous one. Bigger learning rates make it sharper. LoRA-style adapters bolt small new weights onto a frozen base — the base never gets corrupted, so old behaviour is preserved by construction.
build_notes.fix_html: Two moves: (1) replace full fine-tuning with LoRA adapters per data slice — one for support tickets, one for sales, one for compliance; (2) merge adapters at inference, not at training, so any single slice can be swapped or rolled back without retraining the others.
build_notes.metric_html: Kotha et al. measured it: on a 4-slice sequential fine-tune, accuracy on the first slice dropped from 84% → 51% with full FT, but held at 82% with merged LoRAs. Same compute budget.
build_notes.ship_this_week_html: Pick your oldest eval set — the one from your first fine-tune. Run every new model on it before you ship. If the number drops more than 3 points, you're forgetting, not improving. That's your retention metric.

code.body:
from peft import PeftModel

# one adapter per data slice, all merged at inference
base = load_base("claude-opus-4-8")
m = PeftModel.from_pretrained(base, "adapters/support")
m.load_adapter("adapters/sales",      adapter_name="sales")
m.load_adapter("adapters/compliance", adapter_name="compliance")

m.add_weighted_adapter(
    adapters=["support", "sales", "compliance"],
    weights=[0.4, 0.4, 0.2],   # tune per route
    adapter_name="merged",
    combination_type="linear",
)
m.set_adapter("merged")        # base weights untouched → old slices preserved

rows.what_html: Senior IC roles (Staff / Principal AI) are pulling away from manager tracks in Indian comp bands — the curve has steepened sharply this quarter. The market is paying for depth, not headcount.

"Indic-language evals" is the new line item in JDs — basically, you build test sets that prove a model works for Hindi/Tamil/Hinglish users, not just English. Almost nobody has done this in production. If you have, name it.

spotlight.stat_sub: open AI-evaluation roles across Indian product companies and GCCs this quarter — up ~3× from Q1
spotlight.source: Source: NASSCOM AI Talent Tracker, June 2026
spotlight.sodo_html: ↳ So do this: search LinkedIn for "AI evaluation" OR "model evaluation" OR "LLM evals" + (Bengaluru OR Hyderabad OR Pune) and read 5 JDs end-to-end. Start with the careers pages of Razorpay, Sarvam AI, and Flipkart — all three are hiring evaluation engineers right now, and the JDs read like a syllabus.

upskill.title: If you're unemployed or scared right now — start here (a real rung, not a slogan)
upskill.intro_html: You don't need a ₹50k course. Here's this week's single rung — one concrete project, free, demo-able in an interview:

rungs.label: The project
rungs.body_html: Pick one AI assistant you already use. Write 15 prompts in pure Hinglish — the way you actually text a friend. Mix Devanagari and roman script. Include 5 prompts that need code-switching mid-sentence. Run them through Claude 4.8 and one other model. Note every place each one breaks.

rungs.label: Free resource
rungs.body_html: Read AI4Bharat's IndicBench overview (free, on their site) + Anthropic's "evaluating model outputs" cookbook. 75 minutes, total. You'll know more about Indic-language evaluation than 95% of resumes claiming "AI experience".

rungs.label: Done looks like
rungs.body_html: A one-page doc: 15 prompts, side-by-side outputs, and one line per failure on what category of error it is (translation, transliteration, context loss, code-switch). That doc is your portfolio. When they ask "have you worked on Indic AI?" — you open it.

upskill.note_html: Next week's rung builds on this one. We climb together, one issue at a time. Proof beats certificates.

interview.q_label: Question of the week · capability migration framework
interview.q: "Your India-facing support agent handles ~3M tickets a month, 80% in English, 20% in Hinglish that the current model mangles. A new frontier model claims it handles Hinglish without context loss. Do you migrate the 20% — or the whole 100% — and what is the decision based on?"

steps.body_html: Quantify before you decide. Sample 2,000 production tickets and score for code-switching rate, not just language label. Pure Hinglish, pure English, and mid-conversation code-switched are three different cohorts — the eval has to too.

Stratified eval, not benchmarks. Build a holdout from your own tickets, balanced across the three cohorts, scored on resolution-quality not BLEU. The new model has to win on each cohort independently, not on average.

Shadow the segment first. Route the 20% Hinglish stream through the new model in shadow for two weeks. Watch CSAT, escalation rate, resolution time per cohort. Pre-commit the threshold that flips production.

Ramp by segment, not by percentage. Migrate the Hinglish-cohort to 100% before touching English. English migration is a separate decision with its own eval — combining them hides which model broke which cohort.

interview.tip_html: The framework (works for any "new capability claim, heterogeneous user base" decision — new model, new language, new modality, new feature): Segment by user behaviour → build stratified eval per segment → shadow the most affected segment first → ramp by segment, not by percentage. The trap: candidates compare the new model on average benchmarks and roll it out company-wide. Winners name the cohorts, score them independently, shadow the affected one, and refuse to touch the unaffected majority until the affected segment has been validated. Save this — it answers any "should we adopt this for our diverse user base?" question. The meta-skill it tests at any AI lab interview: can you build an iteration loop, or do you just answer the question?

under_the_hood.question_html: Everyone keeps saying models have a "context window." It sounds like a memory size — but a model with a huge window can still miss the thing you wrote three lines ago. So what is a context window actually?
under_the_hood.diagram_svg: <svg class="hood-svg" viewBox="0 0 600 230" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Diagram: a context window is like one whiteboard the model can see all at once. It can read every word, but only weighs a few of them strongly — and once erased between sessions, it remembers nothing."><defs><style>.lbl{font-family:'Archivo Narrow',sans-serif;font-weight:600;font-size:12px;letter-spacing:.08em;text-transform:uppercase;fill:#191712}.sub{font-family:'Archivo',sans-serif;font-size:11px;fill:#5f5c53}.ar{stroke:#9C4A2E;stroke-width:2;fill:none;marker-end:url(#ah3)}</style><marker id="ah3" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="8" refX="6" refY="4" orient="auto"><path d="M0,0 L7,4 L0,8 z" fill="#9C4A2E"/></marker></defs><rect x="20" y="30" width="340" height="140" fill="#ECE7DA" fill="#A9A9A9">to weigh most</text><text x="490" y="148" text-anchor="middle" class="sub" fill="#9C4A2E">size ≠ focus</text><path class="ar" d="M360 100 L396 100"/><text x="300" y="206" text-anchor="middle" class="sub">Bigger whiteboard = more it can <tspan font-style="italic">see</tspan>. It still chooses what to <tspan font-style="italic">notice</tspan>. Wipe the board between chats — gone.</text></svg>

steps.title: So what's actually happening?
steps.body_html: The context window is the whiteboard the model can see at any one moment. Your prompt, the chat history, the documents you pasted — all sit on it. When people say "200k context", they mean a bigger whiteboard, not better eyes. Attention — the mechanism inside the model — then decides which words on that whiteboard to weigh strongly. Size buys room. Attention does the work.

steps.title: Why bigger isn't always better
steps.body_html: A huge context window with lazy attention still misses the line you care about. This is the famous "lost in the middle" problem: stuff at the start and end of a long prompt gets noticed; stuff in the middle quietly doesn't. Stuffing more into the window can dilute the signal, not amplify it.

steps.title: What this means for you
steps.body_html: When the AI misses something obvious from your long prompt, the answer is rarely "give it more." Put the thing that matters at the top or bottom, restate it once, and cut the noise in the middle. Position is a feature you control. Window size isn't.

source.text: Decoded from: Liu et al., "Lost in the Middle: How Language Models Use Long Contexts" (TACL 2024)

the_rep.lite_html: Build the smallest useful thing this weekend: paste your last 5 customer-support emails into Claude 4.8 and ask it to draft a one-line reply for each, in the same tone as your previous replies. Read the drafts. Keep the two that need almost no editing.
the_rep.full_html: Take it one step further: write a 12-line script (or use Claude's project feature) that does this on a schedule — every morning, it reads yesterday's incoming emails and drafts replies into a doc you skim with coffee. You're not building a product. You're building one personal workflow that saves you 30 minutes a day. That's the whole Rep.
the_rep.done: Done looks like: a working draft pipeline you used for one full day, and an honest count of how many drafts were good enough to send with one edit. Reply with the count — featured results are shared with permission.

reader_win.quote: Built the draft pipeline on Saturday. Monday I went through 27 emails in 18 minutes. 14 drafts I sent with one edit. Felt like cheating, in a good way.

toolbox.tool_html: The "two-language gut-check" trick. When you write something important in English — a proposal, a customer message, a policy line — paste it into your AI and ask: "Translate this into simple Hindi the way someone would actually speak it, then translate that Hindi back into English." The round-trip strips out jargon you didn't notice. The version that survives is the version your reader actually understands.
toolbox.try_html: If the Hindi version sounds awkward, your English original is too clever. Plain-language audit, free.

reality_check.h3: The benchmark says "60% better". For Indian-language users, the picture is messier.
reality_check.body_html: Frontier models keep posting big gains on reasoning benchmarks — and those benchmarks are still overwhelmingly English-language, Western-context. The 2025 IndicBench-2 evaluation across 11 Indian languages found that even top-tier models lost 22–38% of their English accuracy on the same reasoning task translated into Hindi, Tamil, or Bengali. The drop was sharpest for low-resource languages — and worst for code-switched (Hinglish-style) input, the way most Indian users actually type. "State-of-the-art" usually means state-of-the-art for English speakers writing English prompts.
reality_check.honest_html: The honest framing: Claude 4.8's Hinglish improvement is real — and still partial. It's not a reason to distrust the model; it's a reason to evaluate it for your users, not someone else's benchmark. The fix isn't doom, it's discipline: build a 100-prompt eval in the languages your users actually speak, and re-run it every time a model upgrades. The bias doesn't go away. It just gets harder to see.
reality_check.source: Source: AI4Bharat & IIIT-Hyderabad, "IndicBench-2: Cross-Lingual Reasoning Evaluation" (2025); language-coverage figures cross-checked against Anthropic's June 2026 model card.

cards.h4: BYJU'S successor Eruditus quietly running AI tutors in 700 Maharashtra schools
cards.body: No flashy launch. A regional pilot with the state board — AI tutors that switch between English and Marathi mid-explanation, based on the student's last reply. One of the largest Marathi-language AI deployments anywhere.
cards.why_you: this is what "AI for the next billion" actually looks like — pedagogy plus code-switching, in classrooms, not on Twitter.

cards.h4: TNEB's solar-grid forecasting AI is now load-balancing 12 GW in real time
cards.body: The Tamil Nadu electricity board has put a homegrown forecasting model into production across its solar belt. Built with IIT-Madras, it's cutting curtailment by an estimated 9% — translating to roughly ₹400 crore a year in avoided losses, and one of the largest energy-AI deployments in Asia.
cards.why_you: India's energy transition is going to be won or lost on forecasting, not panels. This is the boring infrastructure layer where the real work is.

cards.h4: Hyderabad-based Sagar Defence Engineering raises ₹220 cr for autonomous maritime AI
cards.body: Quiet Series B. The company is building autonomous surface and underwater vessels with on-board vision models tuned for Indian Ocean conditions — including the cluttered, low-visibility coastal waters where Western models trained on clean-sea datasets fail.
cards.why_you: India's defence-AI sector just crossed from policy decks to deployed capability. The hiring will follow within 12 months.

india_signal.foot: Spotted something India-only worth a Signal?

closer.body_html: We keep calling these models "frontier" when they cross a benchmark written in someone else's language. A model isn't frontier for India until an autorickshaw driver in Indore can argue with it in his own Hindi — and feel understood. Until then, what we're measuring is how well American AI passes American exams.

foot.reply_prompt: Reply & tell me — did you do the Rep?

decoder.intro: Claude 4.8's announcement is full of context-window vocabulary. Here it is, decoded.

terms.plain: How much the AI can hold in its head at once for a single conversation. Bigger window = more notes it can read before answering. Claude 4.8's is large enough for a small book.
How the AI decides which words in your message matter most. Like which sentence in a long email actually carries the request.
Low-Rank Adaptation. Cheap way to fine-tune a big model — you only update a small slice, so it costs a fraction of full retraining and ships in hours, not weeks.
Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning. The family of techniques (LoRA is one) for adapting a big model without burning the GPU farm.
When teaching an AI a new skill makes it lose an old one. Like editing one chapter of a textbook and accidentally rewriting the previous ones.
Mix of Hindi + English in one sentence. Most Indian internet users write this way; most frontier models still split or break on it.
A standardized test set for Indian-language AI. The benchmark community's way of asking whether a model actually works in Hindi, Tamil, Bengali, and friends.
